---
title: "Signals, Channels, and Noisy Measurements"
description: "A bridge page showing how signals are transformed by channels and sensing systems, and why noise is the common object tying communication and estimation together."
page-type: application
status: published
update-status: current
level: bridge
topic: signal-and-communication
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/probability/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/linear-algebra/index.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain the difference between a signal, a channel, and a measurement process."
  - "Interpret noise as a model for uncertainty, corruption, or information loss."
  - "Recognize why the same signal-channel-noise picture appears in communication and sensing."
  - "Connect application-facing signal language to signal processing, estimation, and information theory."
applications:
  - signal-processing
  - communication
  - sensing
keywords:
  - signal
  - channel
  - noise
  - measurement
  - communication
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: semiannual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
---

## Application Snapshot

A large fraction of communication and sensing can be summarized in one sentence:

`a useful signal is generated, transformed by a channel or measurement system, and observed with corruption`

That sentence already contains the three basic objects:

- `signal`
- `channel`
- `noise`

This page is the shortest bridge from the site's math modules into the language used in communication systems, sensing pipelines, filtering, and inference.

## Problem Setting

A signal may be:

- a time-varying waveform
- a discrete-time sequence
- a vector of transmitted symbols
- an image or sensor field sampled over space

A channel or measurement system then transforms that signal.

At first pass, the picture often looks like

$$
y = Hx + \eta
$$

or, in time-indexed form,

$$
y_t = h(x_t) + \eta_t.
$$

Here:

- $x$ or $x_t$ is the underlying signal
- $H$ or $h$ describes the channel, system, or sensing map
- $\eta$ or $\eta_t$ is noise or corruption

In communication, $x$ may be a transmitted message waveform and $y$ the received waveform.

In sensing, $x$ may be the underlying state or scene and $y$ the measured sensor output.

## Why This Math Appears

This language reuses several math layers already on the site:

- `Linear Algebra`: many channels and sensing systems are modeled as linear maps or matrices
- `Probability`: noise models describe what corruption or uncertainty does to the signal
- `Signal Processing and Estimation`: filtering and denoising try to recover useful structure from corrupted observations
- `Information Theory`: channels are also information bottlenecks with rate and reliability limits
- `Numerical Methods`: reconstruction and inversion often become computational problems, not only formulas

So the signal-channel-noise picture is not a narrow engineering trick. It is the shared translation layer between communication, sensing, filtering, and inference.

## Math Objects In Use

- signal $x$
- received or measured signal $y$
- channel or sensing map $H$ or $h$
- noise $\eta$
- sometimes bandwidth, spectrum, or sampling rate
- sometimes an estimator or decoder trying to recover information from $y$

At first pass, two very common models are:

$$
y = Hx + \eta
$$

and

$$
y = x + \eta.
$$

The first emphasizes transformation plus corruption.

The second emphasizes direct noisy observation.

## A Small Worked Walkthrough

Imagine a transmitter sending a binary symbol sequence through a noisy link.

The ideal transmitted signal is

$$
x \in \{-1,+1\}^n.
$$

The receiver observes

$$
y = x + \eta,
$$

where $\eta$ is random noise from the channel.

Now imagine a sensor measuring temperature with a noisy probe.

The underlying signal is the true temperature trajectory $x_t$, and the sensor outputs

$$
y_t = x_t + \eta_t.
$$

These two settings look different on the surface, but the math objects are strikingly similar:

- there is an underlying signal
- the observation is corrupted
- the downstream task is to recover structure, estimate truth, or decode information

That is why communication and sensing reuse so much of the same math.

## Implementation or Computation Note

Once the signal-channel model is written down, the workflow branches into three practical questions:

1. `Representation`
   What form of the signal should we work with: time domain, frequency domain, symbols, or state variables?

2. `Recovery`
   Are we trying to denoise, estimate hidden structure, detect symbols, or decode a message?

3. `Limits`
   Is the main bottleneck noise, bandwidth, computational cost, or information loss?

Use these pages as the strongest follow-on support:

- [Filtering, Denoising, and Estimation in Communication Systems](filtering-denoising-and-estimation-in-communication-systems.qmd)
- [Signals, Convolution, and Linear Time-Invariant Systems](../../topics/advanced/signal-processing-and-estimation/concepts/signals-convolution-and-linear-time-invariant-systems.qmd)
- [Noise Models, Wiener Filtering, and MMSE Estimation](../../topics/advanced/signal-processing-and-estimation/concepts/noise-models-wiener-filtering-and-mmse-estimation.qmd)
- [Entropy, Cross-Entropy, and KL Divergence](../../topics/advanced/information-theory/concepts/entropy-cross-entropy-and-kl-divergence.qmd)
- [Inverse Problems, Deconvolution, and Regularized Recovery](../../topics/advanced/signal-processing-and-estimation/concepts/inverse-problems-deconvolution-and-regularized-recovery.qmd)

## Failure Modes

- treating the received or measured signal as if it were the underlying truth
- talking about noise as if it only means “small random error,” when it can also represent model mismatch or interference
- forgetting that the same observation model can support very different downstream tasks: decoding, denoising, estimation, or detection
- confusing a channel model with a decoder or estimator
- ignoring whether the important bottleneck is bandwidth, noise level, or recoverability

## Paper Bridge

- [6.011 / Signals, Systems and Inference](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-011-signals-systems-and-inference-spring-2018/) - `First pass` - official MIT bridge where signals, systems, and noisy inference appear in one shared language. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [EE376A / Information Theory](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee376a/) - `Paper bridge` - useful once the signal view turns into channel limits, coding, and information bottlenecks. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [6.003 / Signals and Systems lecture notes](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-003-signals-and-systems-fall-2011/pages/lecture-notes/) - `First pass` - official MIT notes for basic signal and system language. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [6.011 / Signals, Systems and Inference](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/6-011-signals-systems-and-inference-spring-2018/) - `First pass` - official MIT course hub emphasizing noisy signals and inference. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [EE102A course outline](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee102a/CourseOutline.html) - `Second pass` - official Stanford signals-and-systems framing. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [EE278 / Introduction to Statistical Signal Processing](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee278/) - `Second pass` - official Stanford anchor for noise, estimation, and filtering. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [EE376A / Information Theory](https://web.stanford.edu/class/ee376a/) - `Bridge outward` - official Stanford information-theory hub once channel limits and coding become central. Checked `2026-04-25`.
